fr_champagne_bollinger_la_grande_annee.appearance: Medium-deep golden hue with fine, persistent mousse and excellent bead definition. Bright clarity with legs suggesting medium-plus body. The colour is deeper gold than typical NV Champagne, reflecting Pinot Noir dominance and barrel fermentation in old oak.
fr_champagne_bollinger_la_grande_annee.nose_summary: Enticing fragrant aromas of toast, lemon zest and an earthy-smoky nuance typical of the Bollinger house style. Stone fruits including Mirabelle plum and pear are layered with honey, toasted hazelnuts, almond, and vanilla. Red fruit undertones of blackcurrant and raspberry emerge with air. Mocha and fino sherry-like oxidative hints from barrel fermentation. White peony florals on the 2018; mango and exotic fruit on the 2015.
fr_champagne_bollinger_la_grande_annee.palate_summary: Plump, generous and enticing on entry, with flavours of stewed quince, Mirabelle plum jam, and Bosc pear on the 2015. Tropical pineapple and papaya notes on the 2015 vintage. Creamy effervescence with a texture-driven, layered mouthfeel. Toasted walnut, honey, and a dash of bitter marmalade add complexity. The finish blends chocolate and citrus fruits, lengthened by chalky mineral extract and hints of schist.
fr_champagne_bollinger_la_grande_annee.structural_summary: High acidity providing freshness and lift. Medium to full body — more muscular and layered than typical NV Champagne. Fine persistent mousse. 12.5% ABV. Dosage 7-8 g/L (Brut). Dense core of fruit framed by chalky structuring extract. Long, sapid finish. The 2015 shows an old-school generous Bollinger profile; the 2018 reveals citrus freshness and almond.
sources: Wine Advocate (Robert Parker) — 95+ points, 2015 vintage, March 2024
James Suckling — 96 points, 2015 vintage, April 2024
Champagne Bollinger official tasting notes (champagne-bollinger.com) — 2015 and 2018 vintages
Vintus (US importer) — technical notes for 2015 and 2018
JamesSuckling.com — feature article on 2015 LGA and LGA Rosé

es_penedes_recaredo_turo_den_mota.appearance: Surprisingly pale for its extreme age on lees (150+ months). Very fine, delicate bead with persistent mousse. Bright golden hue with green-gold reflexes in younger releases. Clarity is brilliant despite zero dosage and hand disgorgement without freezing.
es_penedes_recaredo_turo_den_mota.nose_summary: Sea spray and chalky minerality lead, with hints of caramelized sugar from brief barrel fermentation. Dried leaves, balsamic bay leaf, and underbrush give a forest-floor complexity. Very fine pastry and marzipan notes from extended lees contact. Fennel, grapefruit, and lime citrus. Some vintages show sherry-like flor reminiscences — spicy curry and fennel notes (especially 2009). Toast, brioche, and subtle smoke in the distance. White flowers and herbaceous notes emerge with air. The wine is described as 'Burgundian and smoky' with great complexity.
es_penedes_recaredo_turo_den_mota.palate_summary: Electric lime citrus fruit juxtaposed against a creamy weight of marzipan from 12+ years on lees. Toast, spice, and smoke remain as supporting characters. Lively and powerful despite the extreme age. The 2004 vintage retains special liveliness — complex and indefinable — with pronounced acidity, intensity, and persistence. Chalky, mineral, and winey character. Rich and sleek texture with coriander notes persisting. Honey on the palate with a lean, structured precision. The wine constantly evolves in the glass.
es_penedes_recaredo_turo_den_mota.structural_summary: High acidity providing remarkable freshness despite 150+ months on lees. Medium-plus body. Very fine bead. Brut Nature — zero dosage, zero added sugar. 11.5-12% ABV. Calcareous soil minerality evident throughout. Long and creamy finish. Balance of concentration and vivacity is the hallmark. Described by Robert Parker's Wine Advocate as 'subtle, elegant, complex and very well integrated' (96+ points, 2004 vintage).
sources: Robert Parker's Wine Advocate — 96+ points (2004 vintage), Luis Gutiérrez
Guía Peñín — 97 points (2004), 99 points (2009)
Decanter — Wine Legend feature on 1999 vintage by Stephen Brook
Recaredo official website (recaredo.com) — tasting notes and vintage descriptions
Atlas Fine Wines — 2009 vintage review with detailed tasting notes

gb_west_sussex_nyetimber_classic_cuvee.appearance: Pale to restrained golden hue — some reviewers note an amber tinge on older disgorgements. Fine, delicate bubbles with a gentle, elegant mousse. Bright clarity. Lighter in colour than vintage Champagne, reflecting the cooler climate and stainless-steel vinification.
gb_west_sussex_nyetimber_classic_cuvee.nose_summary: Prominent toast, buttery brioche, and burnt toast from extended lees ageing (30-36 months). Nutty depth with almond undertones. Fresh-baked white bread. Complex citrus — lemon, green apple — with a hint of pineapple. Caramel and mature baked fruit. An earthy, mushroomy note provides additional complexity (Jancis Robinson). Spicy aromatics. The nose bridges the gap between autolytic richness and cool-climate freshness.
gb_west_sussex_nyetimber_classic_cuvee.palate_summary: Layers of creamy, toasted flavours reminiscent of biscotti dipped in vanilla cream. Honey, almond, pastry, and baked apple provide the core. Fresh apple and citrus (green apple, lemon) add cool-climate energy. Brioche and nutty richness are indulgent without being overpowering. Light- to medium-bodied with crisp green-apple acidity. The finish features caramel and toast flavours with notable length. Intense yet delicate, elegant yet complex.
gb_west_sussex_nyetimber_classic_cuvee.structural_summary: High acidity (8.4-8.5 g/L) — notably higher than most Champagne, giving the wine its characteristic English sparkling tension and freshness. Light to medium body. Fine, soft bubbles — never harsh or overly fizzy. 12% ABV. Residual sugar 8.5-10 g/L (Brut). pH 3.0-3.27. Very long finish. The higher acidity and leaner fruit profile are the key distinguishing markers from Champagne in blind tasting.
sources: Nyetimber official website (nyetimber.com) — producer tasting notes and technical data
Jancis Robinson — 'delicious bakery and fresh-apple aromas, plus a wonderful earthy, mushroomy note'
English Wine Guide (englishwine.blog) — detailed review December 2024
Robert Parker's Wine Advocate — 90 points, noting 'crisp green-apple energy'
Glass of Bubbly — review noting citrus and pineapple on the nose

es_jerez_gonzalez_byass_tio_pepe_fino.appearance: Pale golden yellow — notably light due to the protective blanket of flor preventing oxidation during 4+ years of biological ageing. Brilliant clarity. Legs are light, consistent with the light body and 15% ABV. The colour is a key diagnostic in blind tasting — far paler than Amontillado or Oloroso from the same region.
es_jerez_gonzalez_byass_tio_pepe_fino.nose_summary: Fresh and bright, with prominent notes of lemons, green olives, and a gentle saline touch. Yeasty, bready aromas from flor biological ageing — the hallmark Fino character. Almonds (typical of Palomino), dried flowers, and the softest medicinal/iodine touch. Apple fruit. Sharp yet elegant flor character. The en rama versions add notes of albero sand, esparto grass, and more pronounced nutty undertones. Pungent flor aroma is immediately identifiable.
es_jerez_gonzalez_byass_tio_pepe_fino.palate_summary: Immediately dry — bone-dry with zero sweetness. Savoury and salty, like salted almonds and green olives. Creamy texture despite the dryness. Crisp and fresh when served cold. Marzipan, yeasty brie-cheese-rind notes. Grapefruit acidity and juicy lemon. Medium-dry to medium-bodied on the palate with moderate salinity building toward the finish. Clear acetaldehyde notes. The finish is salty and slightly bitter — a diagnostic Fino marker.
es_jerez_gonzalez_byass_tio_pepe_fino.structural_summary: High acidity providing spine and zest. Light body. 15% ABV (fortified). Bone-dry. Pronounced salinity on the mid-palate and finish. Slight bitter edge on the finish. Long finish driven by citrus acidity and salt rather than fruit. The combination of high acid, salinity, bitter finish, and pungent flor yeast character is the textbook Fino structural fingerprint — unmistakable in blind tasting.
sources: SherryNotes (sherrynotes.com) — Ruben's review of Tío Pepe and Tío Pepe En Rama 2022
González Byass / Tío Pepe official (tiopepe.com) — producer tasting notes
Wine Enthusiast — 89 points, noting 'popping acidity' and 'salty finish'
Wine Advocate (Luis Gutiérrez) — 90-91+ points on en rama versions
88 Bamboo — detailed tasting notes: 'savoury and salty, like salted almonds and green olives'

es_jerez_lustau_don_nuno_oloroso.appearance: Dark bronze colour with a golden rim. Medium-intensity brown with amber highlights and gold glints. Clear and bright. Notably deeper than Fino or Amontillado — the fully oxidative ageing without flor produces the characteristic dark, concentrated colour that is a primary diagnostic for Oloroso in blind tasting.
es_jerez_lustau_don_nuno_oloroso.nose_summary: Pungent nutty aromas — roasted walnut, toasted almond, hazelnut, and marzipan dominate. Smoky wood background. Dried fig, prune, dark caramel, and leather lie underneath. Rich complex of charred wood, maple syrup, mushroom, cardamom, cocoa, and Assam tea. Tobacco leaf, cedar, and warm spice (nutmeg, clove). Booze-soaked plump sultana. Pronounced rancio character. A lemon zing cuts through the oxidative complexity. The nose is intensely aromatic — medium-plus to high intensity.
es_jerez_lustau_don_nuno_oloroso.palate_summary: Full-bodied and concentrated. Rich layers of dried fruit — fig, raisin, prune — drenched in walnut oil, dark chocolate, molasses, and espresso roast. Bitter chocolate, walnuts, and baked chestnuts are the core flavours. Oxidative character brings spicy wood soot and stewed citrus peel. Sultana and some raisin with a cleansing grip. Dry despite the rich, generous texture. Mouthcoating texture. Robert Parker describes 'nuttiness (walnuts) intermixed with a hint of tea and salty, caramelized flavors.'
es_jerez_lustau_don_nuno_oloroso.structural_summary: Medium-plus acidity — providing a tangy, cleansing lift that lightens the concentrated aftertaste. Full body. 20% ABV (fortified higher than Fino to suppress flor). Dry (under 8 g/L RS). Medium-plus flavour intensity. Long, dry, persistent finish with lingering nutty tobacco leaf and spice. The combination of full body, dryness, high alcohol, walnut-rancio character, and tangy acidity is the textbook dry Oloroso structural profile.
sources: Difford's Guide — tasting review sampled April 2025: 'bronze amber with gold glints, pungent walnuts and tobacco'
Robert Parker — 94 points: 'walnuts intermixed with tea, salty caramelized flavors'
Lustau official via Vintage Imports (vntgimports.com) — producer tasting notes
Wine Casual — 94 points: 'roasted walnut, prune, caramel, fig, toffee, butterscotch, nutmeg, toast, marmalade'
Natalie MacLean (Greg Hughes review) — 90 points: 'beautiful amber, charred wood, maple syrup, mushroom, cardamom, cocoa, Assam tea'

pt_douro_taylor_fladgate_20_year_tawny.appearance: Intense amber tawny colour — a characteristic hue between deep ruby and full amber, with bright cherry reflections at the rim. Clear and bright. The colour is diagnostic for aged Tawny — distinctly different from the deep purple-black of young Ruby/Vintage Port, having lost its primary red colour through extended oxidative cask ageing over ~20 years.
pt_douro_taylor_fladgate_20_year_tawny.nose_summary: Opulent and voluptuous nose of complex spicy, jammy, and nutty aromas. Toasted walnut, black espresso, almond, and hazelnut form the nutty core. Hints of orange flower and orange peel. Fine oakiness from the long period in 630-litre seasoned casks. Buttery caramel, dates, and fruitcake. Sweet decomposing autumn leaves with wood notes. Toffee, vanilla bean, and dried citrus rind. Prune and butterscotch. The nose is intensely aromatic and immediately signals aged Tawny Port.
pt_douro_taylor_fladgate_20_year_tawny.palate_summary: Full-bodied with very rich and concentrated flavour. Luxardo maraschino cherry and walnut blend on the palate. Molasses, espresso, Nutella, raisin, and date. Burnt caramel, rich buttery date squares, dried Montmorency cherry, dried apricot, and marmalade. Slightly bitter hazelnut on the finish. The mouthfeel is nicely balanced, smooth, viscous, and rich. Sweet yet with medium-plus acidity preventing cloying sweetness. The flavours linger extensively.
pt_douro_taylor_fladgate_20_year_tawny.structural_summary: Medium-plus acidity (5.3 g/L) providing freshness against the sweetness. Full body. 20% ABV (fortified with grape spirit/aguardente). Sweet — approximately 111 g/L residual sugar. Subtle, integrated tannins — no need for decanting. Long, mellow finish. Smooth, velvety texture. The balance of sweetness, acidity, nuttiness, and oxidative complexity at 20% ABV is the hallmark aged Tawny profile. Serve slightly chilled (12-16°C).
sources: Taylor Fladgate official (taylor.pt) — producer tasting notes and technical data
WineQuest technical sheet — ABV 20%, acidity 5.3 g/L, RS 111.17 g/L
Ken's Wine Guide — 96 points (October 2025): 'toasted walnut, black espresso, Luxardo cherry'
Natalie MacLean (multiple sommeliers) — 90-94 points: 'amber with cherry reflections, buttery caramel, dates, fruitcake'
James Suckling — 95 points: 'dried berries, light chocolate, nuts, honey, toffee, caramel'

pt_madeira_blandys_10_year_bual.appearance: Clear amber colour with tinges of gold on the rim. Medium-deep intensity. The Canteiro-aged colour is characteristic of medium-rich Madeira — darker than Sercial or Verdelho but lighter than Malmsey. Brilliant clarity after fining.
pt_madeira_blandys_10_year_bual.nose_summary: Superbly complex with great intensity. Bouquet of dried fruits — figs, prunes, and raisins dominate. Notes of almonds, oak, and subtle hints of toffee and vanilla spice. Butterscotch. Seville orange marmalade, quince, beeswax, dried orange peel, and cardamom (Wine Advocate). Floral notes. Walnuts and orange confit. Rancio and tea notes develop with age. The nose is immediately identifiable as Madeira through its combination of dried fruit, citrus peel, and the distinctive heat-aged complexity.
pt_madeira_blandys_10_year_bual.palate_summary: Medium sweet and very smooth. Mouth-filling concentrated flavours of maple syrup, figs, caramel, and sugared walnuts. Superb balance of coffee and fruit flavours. Sumptuous texture. Subtle marmalade and spice notes on the entry. Sweet but with a characteristic streak of acid verve that is the Madeira hallmark — like honey with lemon juice. The palate is round, ample, and pleasing. Clean, sharp acidity cuts through the sweetness. Lovely persistent aftertaste that finishes more fresh and dry than sweet.
pt_madeira_blandys_10_year_bual.structural_summary: High acidity (7 g/L total acidity) — significantly higher than Port at comparable sweetness, and the key Madeira identifier in blind tasting. Medium-plus body. 19% ABV (fortified to arrest fermentation). Medium sweet — approximately 95-100 g/L residual sugar. Baumé 3.4. Canteiro ageing (natural attic heat, no estufagem). Endless finish with lovely complexity. The acid-sweetness tension is the diagnostic feature: sweeter than Verdelho but with more acid nerve than Malmsey.
sources: IWSC — Silver Medal: 'Packed with aromas of raisins, butterscotch and vanilla. Maple syrup, figs, caramel and sugared walnuts.'
Wine Advocate (Robert Parker) — 89 points, 2016: 'Seville orange marmalade, quince, beeswax, dried orange peel, cardamom'
Wine Spectator — 91 points: 'amber with gold rim, dried fruits, figs, prunes, almonds, toffee, vanilla spice'
IWC — Silver Medal: 'Floral nose. Walnuts, fig and orange confit. Apricot and marmalade, rancio and tea notes.'
Blandy's official technical sheet (bottlebooks/blandys.com) — technical specifications and IWSC tasting note

hu_tokaj_royal_tokaji_aszu_5_puttonyos.appearance: Light to medium golden colour, clear and bright. Viscous texture visible in the glass — legs are slow and pronounced, reflecting the very high residual sugar (150-160 g/L). Deeper gold in older vintages. The colour is lighter than Sauternes at comparable sweetness levels, a useful blind-tasting discriminator.
hu_tokaj_royal_tokaji_aszu_5_puttonyos.nose_summary: Intense nose with lots of fruitiness and spice. Apricot jam and dried apricot are the signature Tokaji markers. Peach, white peach, and ripe plums. Honey and tangerine. Mango and passion fruit in riper vintages (2018). Ground cherries and slight dill (2016). Ginger, saffron, and marzipan from botrytis influence. Delicate floral aspect. Simple syrup. Fine oak and nutty frame from Hungarian oak cask maturation. Orange rind. The combination of apricot-honey-saffron is the classic Tokaji Aszú aromatic fingerprint.
hu_tokaj_royal_tokaji_aszu_5_puttonyos.palate_summary: Luscious and sensuous with layers of apricot jam, tangerine, honey, ginger, and marzipan. White peach and ripe plum flavours in the 2017 vintage. Orange rind with a slight bitter quality that works well as a counterpoint. Full-bodied and viscous. Rich yet silky on the tongue — not heavy or cloying. Good acidity counters the sweetness, creating the characteristic Tokaji tension. Slight minty note in some vintages. Long finish with a slight bitter orange-rind quality. The wine should drink well for 20+ years.
hu_tokaj_royal_tokaji_aszu_5_puttonyos.structural_summary: Very high acidity (7.9 g/L in 2013; 7.93 g/L in 2017) — dramatically higher than Sauternes (typically 4-5 g/L) and the single most important Tokaji identifier in blind tasting. Medium alcohol at 11% ABV (not fortified — fermentation stopped naturally by sugar concentration). Very high residual sugar — 155-160 g/L typical (minimum 120 g/L for 5 Puttonyos classification). Full body. Long, lingering finish. The acidity-sweetness balance is extraordinary — the wine tastes fresher and less cloying than its RS suggests. Botrytis character adds ginger/saffron complexity absent in non-botrytized sweet wines.
sources: Royal Tokaji official (royal-tokaji.com) — 2017 vintage: 'lots of fruitiness and spice, vibrant freshness, white peach, ripe plums'
Natalie MacLean (Jane Staples, Certified Sommelier) — 95 points, 2017: 'apricot jam, tangerine, honey, ginger, marzipan'
IntoWine — 2016 review: 'peaches, apricots, simple syrup, orange rind, good acidity, long finish'
CellarTracker — 2018: 'ripe stone fruit, tropical fruit, mango/passion fruit, fine oak and nutty frame'
Food Perestroika — 2005/2006 review: 'apricot and honey are the two most recurring Tokaji notes'
